food_name: Chinese Rice Bowl Value Set D
food_subgroup: Chinese rice bowl set meal with protein and vegetable accompaniments

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for a Chinese rice bowl set meal. FOODON_00002809 (rice dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice-based composite dish) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for a Singaporean Chinese rice bowl set meal.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code identified for a Chinese rice bowl set meal composite dish.

main_ingredients: protein (chicken, pork, or tofu depending on selection)

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is approximately one set meal box of 450 g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice, a protein main, and one or two vegetable sides, as typically served at a Chinese rice kitchen in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Chinese mixed rice (economy rice) composite estimate; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for steamed white rice, braised chicken, and stir-fried vegetables
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Value Set D from First Bowl Rice Kitchen is a proprietary set meal. No exact nutrient entry exists in HPB Foodo or USDA for this specific product. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing HPB Foodo data for economy rice (steamed white rice + one meat dish + one vegetable dish) at a typical 450 g serving weight, then back-calculated to per-100 g. The composite reflects approximately 180 g steamed white rice, 120 g braised or stir-fried protein (chicken or pork), and 150 g stir-fried or blanched vegetables with soy-based sauce. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (boiled) and mixed rice-based meal analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for First Bowl Value Set D. GI of 65 is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for boiled white rice (GI ~64–72, mean ~65) as the dominant glycemic component. The composite meal GI is moderated slightly by protein and fat content but remains in the medium range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 78.75) / 100 ≈ 51.2, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 17.5 × 4.5 = 78.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~380 mg per 100 g; ~1710 mg per 450 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this meal to occasional consumption and request reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side options, as a single serving may provide approximately 74% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~51 per serving) driven by steamed white rice as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider reducing the rice portion, substituting brown rice where available, and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.2 g per 100 g) and high sodium (~380 mg per 100 g) from soy-based sauces and cooking oils
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean protein options (e.g., steamed chicken or tofu), request minimal sauce, and limit portion size to manage saturated fat and sodium intake within daily cardiovascular risk reduction targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Rice Bowl Value Set D is a Singaporean Chinese set meal (套餐) served at First Bowl Rice Kitchen, a Chinese rice bowl and bento restaurant at Funan Mall, Singapore. The meal consists of a base of steamed white rice accompanied by a protein main dish (typically braised or stir-fried chicken, pork, or tofu) and one or two vegetable side dishes, all seasoned with soy-based sauces, oyster sauce, or light gravy. This format is closely related to the Singaporean 'economy rice' (经济饭, chap chye png) tradition, where diners select from a spread of pre-cooked dishes to accompany steamed rice. The meal is hearty, savoury, and moderately filling, with a soft rice texture contrasting with tender braised protein and lightly cooked vegetables. Nutritionally, the meal is a moderate source of protein (approximately 35 g per serving) and carbohydrates (approximately 79 g per serving), with a medium glycemic index driven by the white rice component. Sodium content is elevated due to soy sauce and cooking sauces, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load per serving is high (~51), which is clinically relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The meal is dairy-free and typical of everyday Singaporean Chinese lunch and dinner fare. Priced at SGD 13.45, it represents a mid-range set meal in the Singapore food-delivery market.

confidence_reason: Value Set D is a proprietary set meal from a single restaurant (First Bowl Rice Kitchen, Funan). No exact nutrient entry exists in HPB Foodo, USDA, or any approved primary source for this specific product. Nutrient values are estimated by compositing HPB Foodo economy rice data and USDA SR Legacy ingredient data at a representative 450 g serving weight. GI is analogously estimated from University of Sydney GI Database white rice values. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and absence of direct laboratory analysis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on HPB Foodo economy rice data and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries; no direct laboratory analysis is available for this proprietary set meal. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy source data for this food category. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the source data for this composite food category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the source data for this composite food category. 5. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in typical Singaporean Chinese home-style cooking using vegetable oils; confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for comparable dishes. 6. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database white rice values; the composite meal GI may be slightly lower due to protein and fat co-ingestion but is conservatively retained at 65 (medium). 7. Halal status is not confirmed; First Bowl Rice Kitchen is not listed as a MUIS-certified halal establishment as of the processing date. 8. Protein and fat content will vary depending on the specific protein selection within Value Set D (chicken, pork, or tofu). 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and water_g are estimated from HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy composite data and carry medium confidence.
